data.source: Laboratory experiments (undergraduate participants) conducted at Arizona State University; token-based game simulating public infrastructure (bandwidth) and file downloads.
data.wave_or_subset: Treatments included 2-person games and 5-person games (two different 5-person setups); experiments ran from November 2007 to February 2008.
data.sample_size: 10 groups of 2 players (20 participants) and 11 groups of 5 players (55 participants); total 75 participants.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual player (participant) decisions per round; group-level outcomes also analyzed.
data.notes: 10 rounds per session; each player received 10 tokens per round (valued at $0.1 each). Production function for infrastructure is sigmoidal with increasing returns up to 12 tokens and decreasing returns beyond; infrastructure mapped to number of files downloadable. Some treatments included communication and treatments where interruptions could cause loss of partially downloaded files. Data appear to include per-round investments, downloads (files), earnings, and position (upstream/downstream).

method.description: Laboratory N-player token game modeling asymmetric commons dilemmas (irrigation analogue). Players repeatedly decide how many tokens to invest in public infrastructure (bandwidth) and then appropriate resource (download files) according to position in an ordered extraction sequence. The production function for infrastructure is nonlinear (sigmoidal) with threshold effects. Treatments varied group size (N=2, N=5), communication availability, and interruption consequences. Data analyzed using hierarchical linear models, regression analyses, and nonparametric tests.
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants and assign them to groups of either 2 or 5 players.
Run 10 rounds per session. At the start of each round, give each player 10 tokens (worth $0.1 each).
Each round, have players simultaneously decide how many tokens to invest in public infrastructure (bandwidth).
Aggregate investments; compute infrastructure outcome using a sigmoidal (nonlinear, threshold) production function mapping total investment to available bandwidth / number of files downloadable.
Players appropriate resource in an order determined by position (upstream to downstream), downloading files; in some treatments interruptions could cause loss of partially downloaded files.
Allow communication in treatments where specified; allow dynamics over rounds for strategy adaptation.
Record per-round investments, files downloaded per player, earnings, and messages (if applicable).

Analyze data using hierarchical linear models to account for individual and group-level variance, regressions to relate investments/earnings to predictors (position, bandwidth, round), and Wilcoxon matched-pairs tests for pairwise comparisons.
method.models: Hierarchical linear models (multilevel models) to control for individual-within-group variability; regression analyses (unspecified linear models) for tokens earned/invested; Wilcoxon matched-pairs signed-ranks test for pairwise position comparisons.
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes: tokens invested in public infrastructure (per player per round), tokens earned (from downloaded files), number of files downloaded per player.
method.independent_variables: Player position (upstream/downstream), available bandwidth (infrastructure outcome), group size (N=2 vs N=5), round number (time), communication treatment, presence/proportion of economics majors in group, inequality measures (e.g., Gini).
method.control_variables: Group composition variables such as number of economics majors; round fixed effects (time); possibly group-level fixed effects (as per hierarchical modeling).

results.summary: Players made substantial positive investments in infrastructure despite the zero-investment Nash prediction. Upstream players tended to invest more and downloaded/earned more than downstream players, producing unequal earnings across positions. Downstream players reduced investments when upstream players monopolized resources, functioning as a form of costly punishment. Over rounds, investments and earnings tended to increase and inequality (Gini) decreased under coordination and communication. Cooperative (social optimum) outcomes required high collective investment (over 60% of group's endowment) and were harder to achieve in larger groups.

numerical_results.value: difference in investments between A and B
numerical_results.direction: A > B (A invests more than B)
positive (required for social optimum in 5-person groups)
upstream participants earned significantly more than downstream participants

numerical_results.value: A downloaded nearly twice as many files as E
